meta.title: Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: An end-of-day deterministic reflection tool guiding employees through three psychological axes: Locus of Control, Contribution vs Entitlement, and Radius of Concern.

nodes.text: Good evening. You made it to the end of the day. Before you close things out — let's spend a few minutes looking at it honestly. Not to grade yourself. Just to see it clearly.
If you had to pick one word for how today felt — which fits best?
options: Productive
Tough
Mixed
Draining
answer=Productive|Mixed:A1_Q1_HIGH
answer=Tough|Draining:A1_Q1_LOW

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When something worked well today, what do you think made it happen?
options: I had prepared or thought it through beforehand
I adapted when things shifted
Good timing — things just lined up
Someone else came through for me

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When things got hard today, what was your first move?
options: I looked for what I could still control
I pushed through and hoped it would pass
I waited to see what others would do
I felt stuck and couldn't find a way forward
answer=I had prepared or thought it through beforehand|I adapted when things shifted:A1_Q2_INTERNAL
answer=Good timing — things just lined up|Someone else came through for me:A1_Q2_EXTERNAL
answer=I looked for what I could still control|I pushed through and hoped it would pass:A1_Q2_INTERNAL
answer=I waited to see what others would do|I felt stuck and couldn't find a way forward:A1_Q2_EXTERNAL

nodes.text: Think of a moment today when something didn't go as planned. Looking back — did you have any choice in how you responded, even a small one?
options: Yes — I see clearly where I made a call
Probably, but I didn't think about it that way in the moment
It didn't feel like it — the situation was out of my hands
I'm not sure

nodes.text: Think of something that frustrated you today. What made it frustrating?
options: I expected a different outcome and it didn't happen
Other people didn't do what I was counting on
I didn't have the information or resources I needed
Something I thought I'd handled came back
signal=axis1:internal>axis1:external:A1_R_INTERNAL
signal=axis1:external>=axis1:internal:A1_R_EXTERNAL

nodes.text: You stayed in the driver's seat today — even when the road was rough. Recognizing your own hand in what happened isn't about credit. It's about staying connected to what you can actually change. That's a discipline, not a personality trait.

Today pulled your attention outward — to circumstances, to other people, to things that didn't cooperate. That's honest. Hard days do that. But somewhere in there, you made at least one call. Even choosing to wait is a choice. It's worth sitting with: where was your hand in today?
Now let's shift gears. From how you responded today — to what you gave.
Think of one interaction you had today — a meeting, a conversation, a task you shared. Which of these best describes your role in it?
options: I contributed something — effort, an idea, help
I showed up and did what was expected
I felt like I gave more than I got
I was waiting to see what I'd get out of it
answer=I contributed something — effort, an idea, help|I showed up and did what was expected:A2_Q1_CONTRIBUTION
answer=I felt like I gave more than I got|I was waiting to see what I'd get out of it:A2_Q1_ENTITLEMENT

nodes.text: Did you do anything today that wasn't strictly required of you — helping someone, covering something extra, improving a process?
options: Yes — I helped someone with something that wasn't my job
Yes — I did something better than the minimum without being asked
I did my job well, but didn't go beyond it
Not really — today was about keeping my head above water

nodes.text: When you think about what you put in today versus what you got back — what's the feeling?
options: I gave a lot and it wasn't acknowledged
I'm not sure my work is being noticed the way it should be
I deserved better treatment from someone today
I just felt like my effort didn't matter to anyone

nodes.text: When you helped or contributed today — what was driving it?
options: The work needed it and I could do something about it
I wanted the person to be able to move forward
It was expected of me, so I delivered
I wanted to be seen as someone who steps up

nodes.text: Here's a harder question: Did you look for a moment today to give something — without expectation of recognition?
options: Honestly, no — I was focused on my own tasks
I thought about it but didn't act on it
I tried, but I'm not sure it landed
Yes — there was a moment where I just gave, no agenda
signal=axis2:contribution>axis2:entitlement:A2_R_CONTRIBUTION
signal=axis2:entitlement>=axis2:contribution:A2_R_ENTITLEMENT

nodes.text: There's something worth noticing: you gave today. Not because someone asked, not because you'd be measured on it — but because the situation called for it. That's organizational citizenship. It's invisible in most performance systems, but it's what actually holds teams together.

It's worth naming something: a big part of today was about what you were owed, or what wasn't given to you. That's not a character flaw — it's a signal. Usually it means you're depleted, or that something important went unrecognized. But the feeling of being owed has a way of quietly closing us off. The question isn't whether it's fair. It's what you want to walk in with tomorrow.
One more lens. We've looked at your agency and your giving. Now let's zoom out — beyond just you.
When you think about today's biggest challenge or stress — who was in the frame when you were thinking about it?
options: Just me — it was my problem to solve
Me and one other person I was worried about
The team — we were all tangled in it together
Someone who had a harder time than I did
answer=Just me — it was my problem to solve:A3_Q1_SELF
answer=Me and one other person I was worried about|The team — we were all tangled in it together|Someone who had a harder time than I did:A3_Q1_OTHER

nodes.text: That makes sense — some days are head-down, solo work. But even in the midst of your own challenge today, did anyone else's situation cross your mind?
options: Not really — I was too absorbed in my own situation
Briefly, but I couldn't do anything about it
Yes — I noticed someone was struggling even as I was dealing with my own stuff
I was aware of the team but focused on my own part

nodes.text: When you noticed someone else in the picture — what did you do with that awareness?
options: I checked in or did something to help
I acknowledged it but kept my focus
I felt it but wasn't sure what to do
I used it to put my own problems in perspective

nodes.text: Looking at the whole day — who benefited from something you did, even indirectly?
options: Honestly, I'm not sure anyone did today
My direct team, probably
Someone I helped, even in a small way
The end user or customer, even if I didn't see them

nodes.text: Here's a different question: what do you think today looked like from the perspective of someone you worked with? If they had to describe your presence today, what would they say?
options: Focused and reliable — got things done
Helpful and present — noticed what others needed
Stressed or distant — hard to reach today
Collaborative — made the work easier for others
signal=axis3:other>axis3:self:A3_R_ALTROCENTRIC
signal=axis3:self>=axis3:other:A3_R_SELFCENTRIC

nodes.text: You moved through today with your eyes up — aware of others, not just yourself. Maslow called this self-transcendence: the shift from 'what do I need?' to 'what does this situation need from me?' It doesn't mean ignoring your own needs. It means you stayed connected to something bigger than them.

Today was mostly seen through your own lens. Nothing wrong with that — some days demand it. But here's something worth carrying into tomorrow: the most reliable source of meaning at work isn't recognition, or progress, or even doing well. It's connection to someone else's situation. Even one moment of genuine perspective-taking changes the quality of a day.

Here's what today looked like:

On agency, you leaned {axis1.dominant} — {axis1.summary}.

On contribution, you leaned {axis2.dominant} — {axis2.summary}.

On perspective, you leaned {axis3.dominant} — {axis3.summary}.

{closing_reflection}

axis1.internal: seeing your hand in what happened

axis2.entitlement: conscious of what you were owed

closingReflections.internal+contribution+other: That's a strong day — not because everything went well, but because you stayed present, gave something, and saw beyond yourself. That pattern compounds.
closingReflections.internal+contribution+self: You acted with agency and gave today. The next edge: widening the lens to notice how your actions land on others. You have the foundation. Now zoom out.
closingReflections.internal+entitlement+other: You owned your choices today, and you saw others. But something between getting and giving felt unbalanced. The agency you have is real — consider pointing it toward what you can give tomorrow.
closingReflections.internal+entitlement+self: You navigated a lot today. The agency was there. What's worth watching is the weight of what felt owed — it's a signal worth unpacking, not suppressing.
closingReflections.external+contribution+other: You gave and you saw others today, even while feeling knocked around by circumstances. That's actually impressive. The locus work is noticing the small choices you made in between the hard moments.
closingReflections.external+contribution+self: You gave today, even on a hard day. That matters. The next step is connecting the dots: where were your choices? Even small ones. They're there.
closingReflections.external+entitlement+other: Today was heavy in a few directions. You're aware of others, which is something real. Tomorrow, start with one moment: one small choice that's yours, one thing you give before you look at what's coming to you.
closingReflections.external+entitlement+self: Honest days like this have value. You saw it clearly. Tomorrow: one small choice you own, one thing you give without keeping score.

nodes.text: That's it. See you tomorrow.
